issues.title: Hook statement contradicts plant movement
issues.description: The hook says 'Ms. Green [the plant] doesn't move at all.' This is scientifically inaccurate - plants DO move through phototropism and growth movements. This reinforces the common misconception that plants are not living things.
issues.suggestion: Change to 'Ms. Green doesn't run or walk around like a dog, but she does grow toward the light over time.'

issues.title: Contradiction in Mr. Wiggles description
issues.description: The hook says Mr. Wiggles 'moves all by himself' but then asks if he's alive. This is confusing because self-movement IS a characteristic of living things. Students may be confused why something that 'moves by itself' isn't alive.
issues.suggestion: Clarify: 'Mr. Wiggles moves because I wound him up' or 'Mr. Wiggles moves because the batteries push him' to distinguish from self-directed movement.

issues.title: Basic needs inconsistency - shelter missing from objectives
issues.description: Objectives list 3 characteristics (movement, food/air needs, growth) but activities and differentiation materials include 'shelter' as a 4th basic need. The Care Guide activity explicitly includes shelter, creating inconsistency.
issues.suggestion: Update objectives to explicitly include shelter as a 4th basic need, or remove shelter from activities to maintain consistency.

issues.title: Trick question uses untaught concept
issues.description: Quiz question 'Living things can: ... D) Make their own food (trick question!)' - but photosynthesis is NOT taught at Grade 1 level. Students may actually think D is correct since they've been told plants make their own food.
issues.suggestion: Remove 'make their own food' as an option, or explicitly state in instruction that this concept will be taught in later grades.

issues.title: Seed experiment Cup 3 unclear expectation
issues.description: Cup 3 is placed in a 'dark closet' - what's the expected outcome? Students may not understand if it should not grow, grow poorly, or grow differently. No clear prediction guidance.
issues.suggestion: Add explicit expected outcome: 'What will happen? Will it grow tall and green, or will it be pale and weak? Why?'

issues.title: Project assumes access to living thing
issues.description: The summative project requires students to select and investigate a living thing. Students without pets, garden access, or nature access may be disadvantaged.
issues.suggestion: Provide a list of acceptable living things for students to choose from, or offer classroom/school-based options.

issues.title: Objective measurement unclear
issues.description: Objective 1 says '5+ living things with 80% accuracy' - 80% of 5 is 4, but does this mean 4/5 correct? Also '8/10 items' doesn't specify if teacher-provided or student-selected.
issues.suggestion: Clarify: 'Correctly identifies at least 5 living things from a set of 10 (80% = 8/10)'

issues.title: Systems diagram uses advanced vocabulary
issues.description: The systems circle diagram includes 'decomposers' which is a Grade 2+ concept. Grade 1 students won't understand what this means.
issues.suggestion: Simplify to 'Dead things become soil' or remove decomposers entirely.

issues.title: Exit ticket distractor not ambiguous enough
issues.description: Exit ticket A includes 'Moon' as a non-living option - Moon is obviously not living. Better distractor would be 'cloud' or 'shadow' which students might more plausibly confuse.
issues.suggestion: Replace 'Moon' with 'cloud' or 'shadow' for more realistic diagnostic value.

issues.title: Open-ended question has single expected answer
issues.description: Quiz 2 Q4 asks 'What will happen?' to a fish in a bowl with no water. Students might reasonably say 'it will be thirsty', 'it might survive a while', or 'it will get sick' - but rubric expects single answer.
issues.suggestion: Accept multiple reasonable answers or provide specific answer key with alternative responses.

summary: Found 10 issues across scientific accuracy, inconsistent standards, ambiguous instructions, and assessment design. The most critical are the plant movement contradiction in the hook and the shelter/basic needs inconsistency. These could reinforce common misconceptions in young learners.
